coredata.dc:title: GQDs assisted Ba6Co25S27/MWCNT composite as a promising candidate for supercapacitor and a perspective Pt-free counter electrode in DSSC for enhancing the short circuit current
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Gayathri, V.
Muthukumar, A.
Mohan, C. Raja
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Power conversion efficiency
Specific capacitance
Counter electrode and Energy conversion

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of BCS/MWCNT and BCS/MWCNT/GQDs composite
Electrode preparation of energy storage application

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The XRD patterns of BCS, BCS/MWCNT and BCS/MWCNT/GQDs composites.

The SEM image of (a) BCS, (b) BCS/MWCNT, (c) BCS/MWCNT/GQDs composite; (d-g) TEM images of BCS/MWCNT/GQDs composite at differernt magnifications; (h) the fringes in TEM image of BCS/MWCNT/GQDs composite, (i) SAED pattern of the BCS/MWCNT/GQDs composite.
The XPS spectra of (a) Co 2p, (b) Ba 3d, (c) S 2p, (d) C 1 s and (e) O 1 s.
(a) The CV spectra of BCS, BCS/MWCNT and BCS/MWCNT/GQDs CEs, (b) CV spectra of Pt CE, (c) CV spectra of BCS/MWCNT/GQD CE at various scan rates and (d) Cyclic stability of BCS/MWCNT/GQD CE at 70 mV/s.
(a) J-V curve, (b) Tafel polarization curve, and (c) Nyquist plot of BCS, BCS/MWCNT, BCS/MWCNT/GQD and Pt CEs.
IPCE spectra of BCS, BCS/MWCNT and BCS/MWCNT/GQDs CEs.
(a) Cyclic voltammogram of the BCS, BCS/MWCNT and BCS/MWCNT/GQDs CEs at 20 mV/s, and (b) CVs of the BCS/MWCNT/GQDs CE at different scan rates.

(a) GCD studies of the BCS, BCS/MWCNT and BCS/MWCNT/GQDs composites, (b) GCD studies of BCS/MWCNT/GQDs composite at different specific current, (c) Nyquist plots of the BCS, BCS/MWCNT and BCS/MWCNT/GQDs composites and (d) Cyclic stability performance of the BCS/MWCNT/GQDs composite at 15 A/g.
Measured Structural parameters from XRD for BCS, BCS/MWCNT and BCS/MWCNT/GQD CEs.
Measured photovoltaic parameters for BCS, BCS/MWCNT and BCS/MWCNT/GQD CEs.
Comparison of electrochemical performance with recently published ternary metal sulfide-based works.

head.ce:title: GQDs assisted Ba6Co25S27/MWCNT composite as a promising candidate for supercapacitor and a perspective Pt-free counter electrode in DSSC for enhancing the short circuit current

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanostructure Lab, Department of Physics, The Gandhigram Rural Institute, Deemed to be University, Gandhigram 624 302, Tamil Nadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Nanostructure Lab, Department of Physics, The Gandhigram Rural Institute, Deemed to be University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nanostructure Lab, Department of Physics, The Gandhigram Rural Institute – Deemed to be University, Gandhigram 624 302, Tamilnadu, India

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para:
High-efficiency energy conversion and storage materials are the need of the hour in order to tackle the energy problem, along with long-term development using clean energy. Because of their versatility, light weight, and transparency, dye sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) are gaining popularity in many sectors including avionics, with applications ranging from solar-powered drones to origami-style space-based solar power stations [1]. A dye-sensitized TiO2 photoanode, an electrolyte containing the iodide/triiodide (I
-
/I
3
- )redox couple, and a Counter Electrode (CE) catalyzing the reduction of I
3
- to I
2 are all common features of the DSSC [2]. Though researchers focused on photoanode [3–5] the research on CE is essential due to the following reasons. The CEs operate as a catalyst for the reduction process of I
3
- to I
- by transporting electrons from the external circuit into the electrolyte. Up to date, Platinum (Pt) is one of the least reactive metals and has outstanding corrosion resistance at high temperatures. Because of its high electron affinity for the iodine electrolyte and exceptional electrical conductivity, Pt is used as a CE in DSSC [6]. Besides, this significance, the main issue is the employment of costly Pt-based CE. Consequently, the development of low-cost CE materials for DSSC have been employed recently. Therefore, developing a practical and cost-effective replacement has become a priority in terms of minimizing the Pt usage and is being extensively investigated for future development of DSSC. Recently, researchers are focused to find the low-cost earth abundance materials instead of Pt CE such as, transition metal sulfides (TMS) owing to their adaptable crystalline shapes, tunable stoichiometry, and very good catalytic activity [7]. Till now, the alternative CE materials have been grouped into many types namely, metal sulfides chalcogenides consist of binary metal sulfides[8–12], and ternary metal sulfides [13–16]; transition metal nitrides [17,18]; Conducting polymers (PEDOT:PSS) and carbon materials like Multiwalled Carbon Nanotubes (MWCNTs), Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs), reduced Graphene Oxide (rGO) [19–21]. Compared to bimetal sulfides, tri-metal sulfides offers more reduction reaction arising from the co-existence of two cations in a single crystal structure [22,23]. Besides, to increase the conductivity of the metal sulfides based CEs, researchers planned to make the metal sulfide/carbonaceous materials (MWCNT, GQDs, and rGO) composites [24]. Among them, GQDs and MWCNT are the potential candidate for design the composite with metal sulfides owing to their high surface area, superior electrocatalytic activity, excellent corrosion resistance and good synergetic effect. On the surface of GQDs, it is well known that there are numerous negative charged oxygen-containing functional groups [25]. These networks could easily adsorb positive-charged metal ions to modulate each other's structures. GQD decorated mesoporous SrTiO3 delivered 7.16% of Power Conversion Efficiency (PCE), which is largely owing to the more interfacial area helps to easier and fast electrolyte diffusion into the pores, resulting in more I
3
- being involved in the redox reaction [25,26]. Furthermore, the current work’s aim is not only to address the suitable power generation material, also to addressing the efficient energy storage material, which is a big challenge for today’s researchers. Supercapacitor refers to a potential energy storage devices, which has the ability of fast charge/discharge rate, high power density and longer stability [9]. Initially, this field has examined a variety of active materials, with metal oxides initially dominating [27,28]. Currently, TMSs are thought to be more practical substitutes for ternary metal oxides (TMO) in supercapacitors due to their enhanced electrical characteristics, variety of redox processes, high-rate capabilities, and cycle life [29]. Sulfur may exhibit greater flexibility due to its lower electronegativity than oxygen, which facilitates quicker electron passage and is beneficial for electrocatalytic activity. It has recently been discovered that NiCo2S4 offers greater capacity and capacitances than TMO and has a higher conductivity [30]. In recently, Takurou group reported the fabrication of self-rechargeable photovoltaic devices, in which the energy conversion capability exhibited using DSSC and energy storage capability exhibited by supercapacitor [31].

In this work, we describe a strategy to prepare the GQDs decorated BCS composites via facile hydrothermal method and the electrocatalytic properties are analyzed with cyclic voltammetry, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy and Tafel polarization decay under iodine electrolyte. To best of our knowledge, for the first time DSSCs are fabricated with BCS, BCS/MWCNT and BCS/MWCNT/GQDs CEs and the photovoltaic performance also studied. When we employed BCS based composites, GQDs decorated BCS delivered a PCE of 4.38%, being an effective electrocatalyst to those of BCS and BCS/MWCNT CEs, indicates the carbon materials enhance the catalytic activity.

Natural graphite is used as a precursor material for Graphene Oxide (GO) sheets, which are made using a modified Hummer's method [32]. Specifically, 10 mg of GO is added to a beaker containing 20 mL of distilled water (DD water) under stirring. Subsequently, adding the ammonia solution until to reach PH∼10. Additionally, 2 mL hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is also added to the solution as an etching agent. After that, the mixture is transferred to 100 mL of Teflon lined steel autoclave and sealed. The autoclave is then placed in an overheated at 160°C for 5 h allowing the hydrothermally. After cooling, the final colloidal solution is collected and washed with DD water and ethanol (solution A).

In this work, we developed the facile hydrothermal method to synthesis barium cobalt sulfide (BCS) composite for the first time. 0.1 M of cobalt chloride hexahydrate (CoCl2.6 H2O) and 0.1 M of barium chloride dihydrate (BaCl2.2 H2O) are first dissolved in 30 mL of DD water under stirring (solution B). The thiourea solution is also prepared by dissolving the 0.2 M of CH4N2S in 30 mL of DD water (solution C). After achieving the complete dissolving, the solution C is added to solution B under constant and vigorous stirring (solution D). After 15 min, the resultant solution is sealed in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, which was maintained at 180 °C for 24 h, followed by cooling down to room temperature. The obtained products are washed with DD water and ethanol respectively, followed by drying at 80 °C for overnight.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of BCS/MWCNT and BCS/MWCNT/GQDs composite
ce:section.ce:para: Typically, 10 mg of MWCNT is added in the BCS solution under stirring (BCS/MWCNT). Afterwards, the prepared GQDs solution (solution A) to the solution D (BCS/MWCNT/GQDs). Then the final mixture is transferred into Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and kept in oven at 180 °C for 24 h. The final black products are washed with DD water and ethanol and dried at 80 °C for overnight.

Initially, the FTO glass substrates are cleaned with soap solution, DD water and isopropyl alcohol for 10 min. TiO2 precursor solution consisting 40 mM of titanium isopropoxide (TTIP) is dissolved in isopropyl alcohol and the solution is spin coated onto the FTO substrates at 1500 rpm for 30 s, followed by sintering at 450°C for 20 min. Subsequently, the TiO2 paste is prepare using Degussa-P25, DD water, acetyl acetone and Triton-X. The prepared collide is coated on the TiO2 film via doctor blade method and calcinated at 450°C for 20 min in muffle furnace. The cooled TiO2 anodes are immersed in to the ethanol solution of N719 dye (0.5 mM) for 24 h to obtained dye adsorbed TiO2 photoanode. The anodes are rinsed with ethanol solution to remove the weekly adsorbed dye molecules[33].

3 mg of BCS, BCS/MWCNT and BCS/MWCNT/GQDs samples are grained with ethanol using agate mortar to obtain the colloidal solution. Then the solution is dropped on the pre-cleaned FTO substrate. When the ethanol is completely evaporated, the BCS, BCS/MWCNT and BCS/MWCNT/GQDs films are obtained and dried in vacuum at 150°C for 12 h.

Photoanode and CE are used to create the sandwich-like DSSC, which also includes Pt as a comparison. To prevent short-circuiting between the working and CEs, a separator is used to sandwich them together. The I
-
/I
3
- redox electrolyte is injected into the sandwiched cell after the dye sensitized photoanode and CEs had been attached[34].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrode preparation of energy storage application
ce:section.ce:para: The graphite sheet is used as a active materials substrate and Polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) is used as a binder. Firstly, The active material and PTFE were taken in 95:5 ratio and made into a slurry with 10 μl of ethanol. The slurry is then coated on a 1 cm2 graphite sheet and dried at around 80 ºC. The prepared electrode is used as a working electrode in three electrode system.

The crystal system and crystalline nature are analyzed using X-ray diffraction with X′per PRO Model. The shape and size of the sample are analyzed by scanning electron microscope (SEM) (TESCAN VEGA-3 LMU) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Chemical composition and valance states are analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Model: PHI 5000 VersaProbe III). On the workstation (CHI 660 D), electrochemical behaviour is evaluated using a three-electrode setup and 0.1 M of LiClO4, 5 mM of LiI, 0.5 mM of I2, and acetonitrile solution utilizing cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The CV tests are carried out in a three-electrode setup that includes synthesized BCS composites, a saturated Ag/AgCl electrode, and a MWNCT coated graphite sheet that serves as the electrode, reference electrode, and counter electrode, respectively. The photovoltaic measurements are carried out in a solar simulator under the illumination of a 150 W Xe light source combined with standard AM1.5 (100 mW/cm2). The energy storage performance of prepared BCS composites are tested in in electrochemical workstation CHI660D using 6 M KOH.

The crystlline nature of the of synthesized samples is determined for BCS, BCS/MWCNT and BCS/MWCNT/GQDs composites through XRD measurements ( Fig. 1). It is seen that the diffraction pattern are similar and confirming the formation of cubic Ba6Co25S27 nanocrystals (ICDD card No. 81–0585) [35]. The orthorhombic BaS3 phase is matched with the two peaks of the diffraction angles at 34.1˚ and 69.4˚ marked by ♣ (ICDD card No. 74–1059). Furthermore, as evidenced by the sharpness of the peaks, the synthesized products are highly crystalline. Both MWCNT and GQDs are found to be amourphous, as indicated by predominent broad features in the XRD patteren. Moreover, Due to the low content of MWCNT, the diffraction peaks of MWCNT are not visible in the spectra of BCS/MWCNT composite. The structural parameters measured [36,37] XRD for BCS, BCS/MWCNT and BCS/MWCNT/GQD CEs are presented in Table 1.

The shift in lower grazing angle of 0.06°, 0.17 ° is observed after the introduction of MWCNT and MWCNT/GQDs with BCS. Similarly, the shift of grazing about 0.14 ° is observed after the GQDs incorporated with BCS/MWCNT. It is known that the grazing angle is inversely proportional to the d spacing. Hence increase in grazing angle leads to decrease in d spacing. In addition to that the incorporation of MWCNT and GQDs reduce the crystallite size, lattice parameter and increase in dislocation density along with lattice strain. This confirms that the BCS has been successfully modified with both MWCNT and GQDs.

The morphologies of the prepared samples BCS, BCS/MWCNT and BCS/MWCNT/GQDs are shown in Fig. 2.2(a-c). Fig. 2.2(a-b) reveals that the BCS and BCS/MWCNT composite are agglomorated spherical in nature. Fig. 2.2(c) displys the BCS/MWCNT/GQDs composite having rice grains like structure, which is seen in TEM images. TEM images of the BCS/MWCNT/GQDs composite are revealed in Fig. 2.2(d-h). GQDs are decorated by encircling grain rice particles with tiny broken graphene sheets in an alkaline environment using hydrothermal treatment. Furthermore, the TEM image's marked spots aid in understanding the creation and existence of GQDs on the BCS surface.The BCS/MWCNT/GQDs hybrid lattice fringe pattern with an interlayer distance of 0.28 nm matching to the (222) plane for cubic BCS and 0.23 nm for GQDs, which is well compatible with XRD data.

The elemental species of the prepared BCS/MWCNT/GQDs composite is analyzed via X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), which is shown in Fig. 3(a-e). The Gaussian method is used to fit the peaks in high resolution spectra. In the Co 2p spectra shown in Fig. 3(a), there are two spin-orbit doublets and two shakeups are observed. The doublet of Co 2p3/2 (Co3+) and Co 2p1/2 (Co2+) obtained with binding energy 778.1 eV and 780.6 eV, corresponding to the presence of mixed states of Co [38]. Likewise, the binding energy of Ba 3d are observed at 781.1 and 796.7 eV as shown in Fig. 3(b) and these values are corresponds to Ba 3d3/2 and Ba 3d5/2 respectively, with the peak difference 15.6 eV [39,40]. As shown in Fig. 3(c) the binding energy position of S 2p spectra have two peaks at 161.8 and 168.8 eV due to the S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 respectively. C 1 s spectra of BCS/MWCNT/GQDs shows three peaks at 284.6, 283.5 and 288.4 eV relating to C-C, C-O-C and C-CO respectively (Fig. 3(d)). Furthermore, the binding energy for CO, and C–O–C is shown in the peak of O 1 s spectra (Fig. 3(e)) at 532.5, and 533.9 eV, respectively [41].

The electron kinetics and electrochemical ability of the prepared BCS, BCS/MWCNT and BCS/MWCNT/GQDs CEs are analyzed through cyclic voltammogram and Tafel polarization analysis. Fig. 4(a) shows the CV spectra for the all prepared CEs at 20 mV/s and Fig. 4(b) shows the CV spectra of Pt CE for comparision. Fig. 4(c) displays the CV spectra of GQDs incorporated BCS/MWCNT CE at various scan rate (20, 50,70 and 100 mV/s). From Fig. 4(a), one can observed that the two pairs of redox peaks (Ox-1/Red-1; Ox-2/Red-2) reveals the high electrocatalytic activity at the surface of the electordes of the all prepared BCS composites. According to previous literature reports, the first Red-1 peak assigning to I
3
- to I
- reduction and the first Ox-1 peak is ascribed to the oxidation of I
-
/I
3
- , whereas the second Red-2 peak assigning to the reduction of I
2 to I
3
- and the second ox-2 peak is attributed to I
3
- to I
2 oxidation. Peak current density (J
PC ) and peak-to-peak separation (E
PP ) between anodic and cathodic peaks are two important parameters that can indicate the electrode's electrocatalytic activity [29]. The greater J
PC in CEs indicates a faster catalytic response, whereas the lower E
PP indicates a lower over-potential for I
3
-
/I
- reduction. The E
PP is determined by the potential difference between the anodic and cathodic current peaks, and it is inversely related to intrinsic kinetic redox capability. In this work, the obtained E
PP values of BCS, BCS/MWCNT and BCS/MWCNT/GQDs are 0.4, 0.38, 0.35 V. Moreover, the J
PC values of the BCS, BCS/MWCNT, BCS/MWCNT/GQDs and Pt CEs are 3.05, 3.62, 3.99 and 6.01 mA/cm2 respectively.

Among three, GQDs supported BCS/MWCNT CE has the lowest E
PP value of 0.35 V, and J
PC of 3.99 mA/cm2 indicates a greater reduction kinetic ability, implying improved electrocatalytic activity. In addition, the GQDs incorporated BCS/MWCNT CE electrochemical stability has been measured for 40 cycles, as shown in Fig. 4(d).

Fig. 5(a) depicts the J-V curve of as prepared BCS, BCS/MWCNT, BCS/MWCNT/GQDs and Pt CEs sanwiched with dye loaded TiO2 photoanode and I
-
/I
3
- electrolyte. The photovoltaic performance of the fabricated solar cells are calculated using four primary parameters: short-circuit current density (J
SC ), open-circuit voltage (V
OC ), fill factor (FF), and power conversion efficiency (PCE), which are listed in the Table 2. The fabricated cell based on BCS CE exhibited the PCE 2.66% with J
SC of 6.58 mA/cm2, V
OC of 0.75 V and FF of 0.54, whereas, BCS/MWCNT/GQD CE yeilding the PCE of 4.48% with J
SC of 10.57 mA/cm2, V
OC of 0.73 V and FF 0.58. The results are compared to a typical Pt CE (J
SC of 8.54 mA/cm2, V
OC of 0.86 V, FF of 0.71 and PCE of 5.21%). The insertion of GQDs in the BCS/MWCNT composite, which leads to enhancethe J
SC of BCS/MWCNT/GQDs CE (10.57 mA/cm2), which is (38%) higher than the pure BCS. The strong bonding between the BCS/MWCNT interface with low work function, which aids electron delocalization from the BCS to the walls of the MWCNT, contributes to the rise in PCE of BCS/MWCNT/GQDs CEs. Furthermore, because to increased conductivity and electrocatalytic activity, the addition of GQDs to the MWCNT and BCS surfaces facilitates fast electron transport across the electrode/electrolyte interface.

Tafel-polarization analysis is performed on the symmetric dummy cells (CE/electrolyte/CE) employed in the EIS tests to further elucidate the electrocatalytic activity of these CEs. The Tafel polarisation curves for cells based on BCS, BCS/MWCNT, and BCS/MWCNT/GQDs CEs are shown in Fig. 5(b). The exchange current density (J
O ), which is strongly connected with the Charge Transfer Resistance (R
CT ) value, is determined by the tangent slope of the Tafel curves, as given in the relation:
J
0
=
R T
n F
R
C T
. Where R is gas constant, T is the absolute temperature, F is the Faraday constant, and n is the number of electrons participate in the reduction process respectively. A greater J
O indicated a stronger catalytic activity of the CE catalyst, while a larger slope indicated a steeper slope[42]. The J
O value of BCS/MWCNT/GQDs CE is higher than the BCS, BCS/MWCNT CEs (Fig. 5(b)). Furthermore, the limiting diffusion current density (J
lim ) is the cathodic branch intercept on the y- axis of the diffusion zone. J
lim is proportional to the diffusion coefficient of I
3
- in the redox electrolyte, therefore a larger J
lim signified a large diffusion coefficient[43]. As shown in Fig. 5(b) the higher J
lim of BCS/MWCNT/GQDs CE indicates that the higher diffusion co-efficient of I
3
- . As a result, the catalytic activity of Pt and BCS/MWCNT/GQD trended in the same direction as the CV and J–V values mentioned earlier.

As stated in previous disscusion of Tafel analysis, EIS investigations are carried out utilising the symmetr ical dummy shift to Tafel analysis test cells. Basically, the nyquist plot for a DSSC consist of three semicircles[44]. The Nernst diffusion of I
3
- inside the electrolyte is responsible for the tiny semicircle found in the MHz range, the charge transfer resistance (R
CT2 ) at the TiO2/dye/electrolyte interface is responsible for the prominent big semicircle observed [45] in the intermediate frequency range (10–100 Hz), the semicircle in the high-frequency region (kHz) is ascribed to the charge transfer resistance (R
CT1 ) at the CE/electrolyte interface[46,47]. Ussing the ZSIM programme, all of the EIS plots are fitted using an equivalent circuit model, and the resulting EIS parameters are displayed in Fig. 5(c). The Series Resistance (Rs) of the interfacial resistance between the electrode materials and the FTO conducting glass substrate is measured using the semicircle at the opening point of the high-frequency region and the diameter of the semicirle is named as the Charge Transfer Resistance (R
CT ). As shown in Table 3, the R
CT values of the BCS, BCS/MWCT, BCS/MWCNT/GQDs and Pt CEs are 729.7, 523.1, 163.1 and 27.3 Ω respectively. The lowest R
CT was associated with a faster chargetransfer kinetics, which might be attributed to the CE's bigger electrocatalytically active area. In comparison, BCS/MWCNT/GQDs CE shows the lower R
CT than BCS and BCS/MWCNT CEs, which may due to the decoration of GQDs on the surface of BCS and MWCNTs, that serves the dominent electron pathway for electron conduction and the synergistic interaction between the BCS/MWCNT and GQDs.

Fig. 6 compares the IPCE spectra of DSSC for the BCS, BCS/MWCNT and BCS/MWCNT/GQDs CEs in the wavelength range 400–800 nm. The IPCE peak values of the prepared CEs are well identified at ∼510 nm respectively. From Fig. 6 we can observe that the GQDs embedded BCS/MWCNT CE shows the highest absorption of 41.7% in the 420–600 nm, which is 24% higher than the BCS/MWCNT CE. JSC was shown to be favourably related to the IPCE value. As a result, the overall IPCE study result corresponded to the changes in the JSC seen in the J-V curve analysis. This indicates the GQDs incorporated BCS/MWCNT CE shows the good catalytic property, and it is promising replacement of Pt CE material for DSSC.

To verify the electrocatalytic behavior of the BCS, BCS/MWCNT and BCS/MWCNT/GQD composites, using three electrode configuration the CV test are performed between the potential window − 1.0 to 0.3 V with 6 M KOH and the results are displayed in Fig. 7(a). In all the three electrodes, the CV reals the oxidation and reduction peak. The integrated CV area of the GQDs supported BCS/MWCNT electrode is greater than that of the pristine BCS and BCS/MWCNT electrode, implying capacitance improvement due to the synergistic impact of good MWCNT conductivity. The CV spectra of BCS, BCS/MWCNT and BCS/MWCNT/GQDs electrodes shows the quasi-rectangular shape which indicates the pesudocapacitor behavior. Fig. 7(b) shows the CV spectra of BCS/MWCNT/GQDs electrode at different scan rates (20, 50, 70 and 100 mV/s).

To assess the samples' real applicability for supercapacitors, GCD tests are carried out in the − 1.0 to 0.3 V (versus Ag/AgCl) window. In lithium-ion batteries, the CoS2 has been used as a possible photoanode material. As a result, there's some debate about whether this is a capacitive or battery-like activity. The difference between the pesudocapacitor and battery type materials has clearly explained by Thierry Brousse et al., and clarified the obscurities[48]. Despite this basic definition, some faradaic-behaving battery-type materials have been designated as "pseudocapacitive" (F/g) rather than "capacity" (C/g). According to Patrice Simon et al., when battery materials are manufactured at the nanoscale, the power density is increased owing to the short ion transport routes[49]. Fig. 8(a) displays a sloping plateau in the GCD spectra of the BCS-based electrode, indicating a combined state of capacitance behavior with a regular triangular profile and ending with capacity (battery-like action) with a flat discharge curve. Subsequently, the corresponding discharge curves are used to measure the respective specific capacity (Cs) values according to the relation:
C
s
=
I ∆ t
m
, where ∆t is the discharging time interval, I is the applied current and m is the mass of active material. The calculated specific capacity values of the BCS, BCS/MWCNT and BCS/MWCNT/GQDs electrodes are 105, 180 and 191 C/g. Among the synthesized set of samples, the GQDs incorporated BCS/MWCNT shows the threefold increases in the capacitive performance than the bare BCS composite due to (1) addition of GQDs which increase the surface area creates more active sites, (2) interconnection between the MWCNT and BCS facilitate the fast electron transport. Fig. 8(b) reveals that the GCD spectra of the BCS/MWCNT/GQDs composite at various specific current of 2, 4, 6, 8 and 10 A/g respectively. The acquired result is evaluated against the newly released ternary metal sulfide-based works shown in Table 4. Moreover, the resistive and capacitive behavior of the prepared electrodes are diagnosed by a.c impedance analysis (Fig. 8(c)). Fig. 8(c) shows the lower R
CT value revealing the enhancement in the conductivity of the BCS/MWCNT composite by the incorporation of GQDs and helps to improve the synergistic effect of the BCS and MWCNT. The estimated R
CT values of the BCS, BCS/MWCNT and BCS/MWCNT/GQDs electrodes are 116.2, 25.3, 22.4 Ω respectively. Furthermore, cycle life of the BCS/MWCNT/GQDs electrode is checked to examine the practical utility of the electrodes over 1000 continuous charge/discharge cycles at much larger current density of 15 A/g. Fig. 8(d) reveals the 67.5% capacitive retention is gained at the end of the results.

Solar cells containing BCS/MWCNT/GQDs composite CEs are constructed for the first time, and the influence of GQDs on solar cell performance is investigated. The BCS, BCS/MWCNT and BCS/MWCNT/GQDs composites are synthesized by facile hydrothermal method. The XRD pattern confirms the formation of cubic Ba6Co25S27 nanocrystals. BCS, BCS/MWCNT and BCS/MWCNT/GQDs composites are spherical in nature and SAED pattern helps to understand the crystalline nature of the prepared samples. The elemental composition and chemical state of the BCS/MWCNT/GQDs samples are analyzed using XPS. The results of cyclic voltammetry and Tafel polarization demonstrate that the BCS/MWCNT/GQDs composites has a stronger electrocatalytic activity and quicker reaction kinetics. J-V spectra shows that the BCS/MWCNT/GQDs cell delivered the highest Jsc of 10.57 mA/cm2 and a PCE of 4.48% which is 41% higher compared to the pristine cell. In addition, the supercapacitor performance under KOH electrolyte is examined using the same samples as a working electrode. Among three, the GQD embedded BCS/MWCNT composite as a working electrode in the supercapacitor gives a high specific capacity of 191 C/g. Moreover, the BCS/MWCNT/GQDs electrode shows the 67.5% capacity retention even after 1000 continuous charge/discharge cycles. As a result, the BCS nanostructure supported by GQDs is an ideal electrode material for energy conversion and storage devices.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper
